coredata.dc:title: Polyethyleneimine modified fluorescent carbon dots and their application in cell labeling
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Wang, Wenxing
Wu, Hongyan
Fang, Fang
Wang, Naizhi
Zhang, Xiujuan
Xu, Shukun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were solvothermaly synthesized in water–glycol medium by using glucose as carbon source and then modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) for the first time to improve fluorescence quality. The as-prepared CDs were monodispersed sphere particles with a diameter of about 7.5nm, emitting strong fluorescence which is excitation wavelength-dependent, with a quantum yield of 3.5%. After PEI modification, there was a 300-fold enhancement in fluorescence intensity and also a red shift in emission wavelength. The as-prepared CDs were then conjugated with CEA8 antibody to label and image HeLa cells in vitro. We also tested the cytotoxicity of these CDs using HeLa cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, demonstrating much better biosafety property compared with CdTe quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Solvothermal
Polyethyleneimine
Cell labeling
Cytotoxicity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) image of as-prepared CDs as well as the size distribution (c) and size statistics (d) reported by intensity.
The IR spectra of PEI-modified (a) and bare (b) CDs.

The emission spectra of bare (a) and PEI-modified (b) CDs obtained at the excitation wavelength of 360–440nm with 20nm increase. The PEI-modified CDs were diluted to 100-fold whereas the bare CDs without dilution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

Excitation, emission (a) and absorption (b) spectra of the CDs before and after PEI-modification. The solid line represents spectrum of PEI-modified CDs, while dotted line for bare CDs. The inset digital photos in (a) are fluorescent images of bare (left) and PEI-modified (right) CDs.
Fluorescence intensity of PEI-modified and bare CDs with different pH. The PEI-modified CDs were diluted 100-fold, whereas the bare CDs were without dilution.

Image of HeLa cells incubated alone (a), co-incubated with CDs (b) and co-incubated with CDs-CEA8 (c). In the three panels, the left row is imaged in bright field; the central row represents the fluorescence images excited by UV; the right is the overlay of the image in the left and central row.
The viability of HeLa cells co-incubated with CDs and CdTe QDs of different concentration for 6h (a) and co-incubated for different time at 0.75mg/mL of CDs and 0.1mg/mL of CdTe QDs (b).

head.ce:title: Polyethyleneimine modified fluorescent carbon dots and their application in cell labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biological Technology, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were modified with PEI to improve fluorescence quality and then conjugated with CEA8 antibody to label and image HeLa cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, demonstrating much better biosafety property compared with CdTe quantum dots.

► Carbon dots were firstly modified with PEI to improve fluorescence performance. ► PEI modified CDs were conjugated with CEA8 antibody to label and image HeLa cells. ► Biotoxicity of the CDs was studied and firstly compared with that of CdTe QDs.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were solvothermaly synthesized in water–glycol medium by using glucose as carbon source and then modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) for the first time to improve fluorescence quality. The as-prepared CDs were monodispersed sphere particles with a diameter of about 7.5nm, emitting strong fluorescence which is excitation wavelength-dependent, with a quantum yield of 3.5%. After PEI modification, there was a 300-fold enhancement in fluorescence intensity and also a red shift in emission wavelength. The as-prepared CDs were then conjugated with CEA8 antibody to label and image HeLa cells in vitro. We also tested the cytotoxicity of these CDs using HeLa cells. No apparent cytotoxicity was observed, demonstrating much better biosafety property compared with CdTe quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence semiconductor nanocrystals such as CdTe, CdSe, CdS and their core-shell structure have attracted great attention due to their excellent performance as fluorescence probes [1–4]. However, the biotoxicity of the heavy metal, cadmium, the main ingredient of fluorescence semiconductor nanocrystals, is a major concern for their bio-applications [5,6]. Therefore, there still exists the need to search for fluorescence nanoparticles with similar or better photoluminescence (PL) and biosafety property.

Carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotube [7], carbon nanohorn [8], graphite [9] and nanodiamond [10] have been widely applied in the field of chemistry, physics and materials. Recently, another new carbon-based fluorescence material, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), was proposed as a new type of imaging reagent [11–13]. Usually, CDs are of 2–10nm in diameter, non-photobleaching and easy to prepare, with good fluorescent performance, excellent dispersity and biocompatibility [11,13–16]. Common routes for preparing fluorescent CDs include oxidation of candle soot with nitric acid [17–19], electrolysis of multi-walled carbon nanotubes [20], oxidization of carbon nanotubes by mixed acid [21], collecting from high-energy ion beam radiation of graphite [22], carbonization of organics [23–25], ultrasonic synthesis [26], etc. All carbon dots prepared as stated above showed great photoluminescence. However, most of these methods were either tedious in process or required expensive device. Therefore, a simple method of synthesizing CDs with excellent property remains challenging.

Usually, surface modification of CDs is necessary for their biological applications. Recently there have been new reports [25,27] on the fluorescence behavior of non-modified carbon nanoparticles prepared from lamp soot and their application in the imaging of normal and cancer cells. In this report, cell labeling was achieved through cellular uptake of CDs. Several agents have been reported for CDs modification, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [20,28,29], poly (ethylenimide)-co-poly (ethylene glycol)-co-poly(ethyl-enimide) (PPEI-EI) [22], 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13- tridecanediamine (TTDDA) [23], citrate and the 4-aminoantipyrine [24], etc. One recent study reported the synthesis of water-soluble CDs by a one-step hydrothermal synthetic route using acid/alkali as additives [30]. These CDs emit bright photoluminescence covering the entire visible-near infrared (NIR) spectral range. The CDs also have up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) properties, while the NIR PL of the CDs can also be observed by NIR excitation. Control experiments indicated that different additives can strongly affect the PL properties of the CDs. Herein, we report a facile, economic and green solvothermal method toward the synthesis of bare CDs, which was then modified by PEI for the first time, resulting the quantum yield of 3.5%.

The objective of this study is three-fold: (1) to demonstrate that modification with PEI can improve CDs fluorescence property; (2) to achieve the immunolabelling of HeLa cells through the conjugation of PEI-modified CDs with CEA8 antibodies; (3) to investigate the biotoxicity of these CDs when used as cell-labeling agent in vitro and compare with quantum dots (QDs).

Thioglycolic acid, sodium borohydride (96%), tellurium powder (99.999%), CdCl2·2.5H2O, glycol and glucose were purchased from National Medicines Corporation Ltd., China. Branched polyethyleneimine (50%, v/v) was purchased from Aldrich–Sigma Chemical Co. Ltd., USA. MTT was purchased from Amresco, USA. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylamino-propyl)-3-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%) were purchased from Acros Organics, NJ, USA. RPMI 1640 medium was purchased from GIBCO Company, USA. CEA8 antibody was purchased from Beijing biosynthesis biotechnology Co. Ltd., China. HeLa cells were supplied by China Medical University. All the aqueous solutions were prepared using double distilled water.

The size and morphology of prepared CDs were characterized by using a JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan) with an accelerating voltage at 100kV. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were acquired using an F-320 Luminescence Spectrometer (Gangdong Science and Technology, China) equipped with a xenon arc lamp as the light source. UV absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2100 UV/Vis spectrometer (Ruili Analytical Instrument Company, Beijing, China). Both the fluorescence and UV absorption spectra were measured with a 1cm path length cuvette. The size distribution of prepared CDs was measured by mean of Mastersizer 2000 zetasizer (Malvern, UK). The infrared spectroscopy was recorded by using a Spectrum One (B) Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA).

First, 0.5g of glucose was added to 25mL mixed solution of glycol/distilled water (v:v/1:1) to form clear solution with ultrasound. Then, the mixture was added to a high pressure nitrifying pot (45mL) and heated at 180°C in a constant-temperature drying oven (HN101-2, Shanghai Sujin instrument equipment factory, China) for 2.75h to form bare CDs. Finally, 1.0mL of PEI solution of 20% (v/v) was mixed with 25mL of the bare CDs solution and stirred for 3h at 80°C. Color change of the solution to dark yellow and the emission of strong fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp indicated the formation of PEI modified CDs.

The CdTe QDs used for the comparison with CDs in the following cell viability study (Section 2.6) was synthesized using the reaction between Cd2+ and NaHTe solution following a method described previously [31], with TGA as a stabilizing agent. Briefly, the sodium borohydride was reacted with tellurium powder (3:1 molar ratio) in water to produce NaHTe. The molar ratio of Cd2+:Te2−:TGA of 1:0.5:2.4 was used. The CdTe QDs emitting green fluorescence were obtained through hydrothermally heating at 140°C for 60min.

Immunolabeling of HeLa cells by the CDs was carried out mostly according to our previous work [32]. First, CEA8 antibody was conjugated with PEI-modified CDs via the activation of EDC and NHS. Typically 100μL of NHS and 100μL of EDC (both of 0.2mg/mL) were added to 1mL of 0.75mg/mL CDs solution, and then activated at room temperature for 10min. The mixture solution was added to 400μL of 0.2mg/mL CEA8 antibodies, and the reaction was carried out for 2h at 37°C with oscillating. HeLa cells were co-incubated with CDs-CEA8 mixture solution at 37°C and 5% CO2 for 1.5h. Before cell-imaging, these cells were washed by PBS buffer to remove unbound CDs. Lastly, fluorescent microscope images were acquired by Leica DMIL inverted ﬂuorescence microscope with ultraviolet excitation and attached CCD system. Blank experiment and check experiment were also carried out at the same time by incubation of HeLa cells only without CDs, and co-incubation of the cells with the PEI modified CDs, respectively.

HeLa cells were also used to study the cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs. The cell viability assay was performed by MTT, which determining absorbance at 470nm to explain the cells viability [33]. HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated in culture medium for 24h at 37°C in 5% CO2. The culture medium was then removed and 300μL of 0.75mg/mL CDs in PBS was added to every well and co-incubated with the cells for 1.5, 3, 4.5 and 6h, respectively, to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs with different incubation time. Cellular cytotoxicity associated with different concentration of CDs was also investigated by adding 300μL of CDs of 0.5, 0.75, 1.0 and 1.25mg/mL to every well of a new 96-well plate with HeLa cells seeded at the same density as above and incubated for 6h. As a comparison, viability of HeLa cells in the presence of CdTe QDs, prepared in Section 2.4, was measured in the presence of 0.075, 0.1, 0.125, 0.15mg/mL CdTe QDs for 6h and co-incubating with CdTe QDs of 0.1mg/mL for 1.5, 3, 4.5 and 6h respectively, and the results were compared with the above data from cell viability study with CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the TEM images of as-prepared CDs showed that the prepared CDs were uniform, monodispersed sphere particles with the approximate diameter of about 7.5nm. The lattice structure was discernible on the HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) further confirming the formation of CDs. The size distribution of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1c and d.

The surface groups of bare and modified CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrum (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, 1619cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of acyl group (CONR); the narrow and strong peak (3416cm−1) corresponded to the stretching vibration of amino-group, proving the binding of PEI to the surface of bare CDs. For the bare CDs (Fig. 2b), characteristic stretching vibration of CO (1633cm−1) and broad stretching vibration of OH (3387cm−1) were also observed.

Both the bare and PEI-modified CDs have broad range of emission wavelengths and the emission wavelengths depend on the excitation wavelengths. The CDs can exhibit blue, green, and yellow photoluminescence under different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 3 shows that both the bare and the modified CDs possess multicolor luminescence as reported previously [28,29]. For the bare CDs, when the fluorescence spectrum was measured from 360 to 440nm with 20nm increment, the emission wavelength ranged from 450nm (blue) to 515nm (reseda) as shown in Fig. 3a. Whereas for the PEI-modified CDs, when excited from 360 to 440nm with 20nm increment, the fluorescence spectra ranged from 470 (pale blue) to 535nm (green) as clearly shown in Fig. 3b. Comparing to the bare CDs, there is a 20nm red shift for the PEI-modified CDs, under the same exciting wavelength.

It is obvious from Fig. 4a that the maximums excitation wavelengths of the two CDs (both of them were diluted to 100 times, monitored at 475nm) are both located at 350nm, whereas there is a 300-fold difference in fluorescence intensity. Similar degree of difference was also observed in their emission spectra (both of them were diluted to 100 times, monitored at 350nm) as shown in Fig. 4b, which also includes the digital images of CDs solutions (insert) taken under 365nm UV lamp. The emission intensity was also enhanced by about 300-fold through PEI modification, companying with a red shift of maximum emission position. It is clear from their UV absorption spectra (Fig. 4c, both of them were diluted 30-fold) that there is an obvious absorption peak at 360nm for PEI-modified CDs which is absent for the bare CDs, which corresponds to the maximum fluorescence excitation spectrum.

Acidity of medium is an important influence factor on the photoluminescence of CDs. Our experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensities of both CDs decreased with the increase of pH value (Fig. 5 ). There is an 82% reduction in fluorescence intensity of bare CDs when pH increased from 3.0 to 12.0. In contrast, fluorescence intensity of PEI-modified CDs in the same pH range only fell by 25%. This result suggests that the CDs modified by PEI have a wider pH stability range for optimal fluorescence emission. For example, from pH 3 to pH 8, the reduction of fluorescence intensity is only 11%.

To sum up, the experimental results show that both the bare and the PEI-modified CDs have fluorescence emission, and there was no apparent change in fluorescence intensity for more than two months for both (data not shown). Whereas for the CDs modified by PEI, there were enhancements in UV absorption, fluorescence intensity and obvious red-shift in maximum wavelength. In addition, they showed better photostability in a wider acidity range of pH 3-8. One hypothesis for the observations is that the tethered modifier stabilizes the surface of the carbon nanoparticles, facilitating the generation of energy traps that emit light when stimulated, an effect described as emission from modified surfaces.

Due to the excellent fluorescent characteristics, the CDs could be used to target specific cells by using cell-specific antibody or other reagent. Here, the as-prepared PEI-modified CDs were used as a probe to label HeLa cells through conjugation with CEA8 antibody. After modification with PEI, amino-group on the surface of as-prepared CDs can be linked with CEA8 antibody upon activation with EDC and NHS, without any further functionalization. The images of HelLa cells labeled with antibody conjugated CDs (0.75mg/mL, incubated for 1.5h) are shown in Fig. 6c. The green contour of cell shape was apparent under a fluorescence microscope equipped with UV excitation. The image of blank control experiment did not show any autofluorescence with UV excitation (Fig. 6a). In addition, HeLa cells incubated with CDs without CEA8 antibody also did not show any fluorescence (Fig. 6b). Both results demonstrated the specificity of PEI-modified CDs in HeLa cell labeling.

Biocompatibility of CDs is one of the most important properties to consider for their application in cell-labeling and imaging [27,34]. Here, we used HeLa cells to investigate the cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs. CdTe QDs were also included in the experiment as a comparison. As show in Fig. 7a, there is a minor reduction in the viability of HeLa cells after 6h incubation with increasing concentration of CDs. In contrast, HeLa cells incubated with CdTe QDs had much more reduction in cell viability with increasing concentration. Fig. 7b illustrates the viability of HeLa cells under different incubation time. It is clear that the cytotoxicity of both the as-prepared CDs and the CdTe QDs was incubation-time dependent. Consistent with the observation in the dose response experiment above, CdTe QDs again showed significantly more reduction in cell viability compared with PEI modified CDs. It is notable that in our experiment of cell labeling and imaging, the concentration of as-prepared CDs was 0.75mg/mL and incubation time was 1.5h, with this condition, the cell viability was 99.3%, showed an ignorable cytotoxicity.

Fluorescence carbon dots with strong fluorescence were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method and then modified using PEI for the enhancement of luminescent intensity. The fluorescence properties of PEI-modified CDs were markedly improved compared with bare CDs. Moreover, these CDs were stable within a much wider pH range of 3–8 compared with bare CDs. The as-prepared CDs were successfully used to target HeLa cells through conjugation with CEA8 antibody. Cytotoxicity experiment of as-prepared CDs only showed minor effect on HeLa cell viability and significantly better biocompatibility compared with CdTe QDs. In addition, the concentration and incubation time needed for cell-labeling and imaging in vitro, are much lower than the conditions used for cytotoxicity testing. Taking into account of the advantages of carbon dots, this class of PEI-modified CDs could serve as a promising candidate for fluorescence probes used in in vitro and in vivo applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 20875011), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N100405014 and N110805001), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (20100481205).
